skill.name: skill-creator
skill.description: Guides creation and maintenance of AI skills with reusable instructions, bundled resources, validation scripts, and packaging workflows.
skill.summary: Create clear, reusable AI skills with templates, validation, and packaging guidance.
skill.author: DaoThiHuong2111

security_audit.summary: The static high-risk weak-cryptography and external-command findings are false positives from license text, Markdown backticks, and template prose. No prompt-injection language, network requests, credential access, or malicious intent was found. The remaining risk is medium because bundled scripts perform local filesystem writes and recursive ZIP packaging with limited input validation.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Path Component in Skill Initialization
medium_findings.description: The initializer appends the user-supplied skill name to the resolved output path before creating files. The help text documents hyphen-case requirements, but the script does not enforce them before filesystem writes, so an untrusted name with path separators or parent directory segments could write outside the intended directory.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are clear and the skill name reaches the path construction without validation. Impact depends on a user running the script with untrusted arguments, so this is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Recursive Packaging Can Include Unintended Files
medium_findings.description: The packager recursively adds every file under the selected skill folder to the output archive after minimal validation. If the folder contains secrets, private notes, hidden files, or build artifacts, they can be distributed unintentionally.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The archive loop is direct and unfiltered, which creates a realistic accidental disclosure risk. There is no evidence of network exfiltration or malicious intent, so the confidence is high for the behavior but moderate for exploitability.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner matched Markdown backticks and template text that describe command usage and resource names. No evidence found of subprocess calls, os.system usage, shell execution, eval, or Ruby-style backtick execution in the reviewed Python files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown examples or Python string templates, not executable shell APIs. The executable scripts use pathlib and zipfile rather than command execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts occur in license prose, Markdown descriptions, examples, and validation text. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or other weak cryptographic algorithm calls used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched locations are ordinary text or regular-expression validation, not cryptographic implementation. Review found no cryptography imports or security-sensitive hashing behavior.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Detections Are License References
low_findings.description: The only hardcoded URLs identified are Apache License reference links. No evidence found of active network requests, callbacks, telemetry, downloads, or data exfiltration code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URL locations are standard Apache License text. The reviewed scripts do not import HTTP libraries or perform network operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unvalidated Path Component in Skill Initialization
dangerous_patterns.description: The initializer appends the user-supplied skill name to the resolved output path before creating files. The help text documents hyphen-case requirements, but the script does not enforce them before filesystem writes, so an untrusted name with path separators or parent directory segments could write outside the intended directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem writes are clear and the skill name reaches the path construction without validation. Impact depends on a user running the script with untrusted arguments, so this is medium rather than high.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Packaging Can Include Unintended Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The packager recursively adds every file under the selected skill folder to the output archive after minimal validation. If the folder contains secrets, private notes, hidden files, or build artifacts, they can be distributed unintentionally.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The archive loop is direct and unfiltered, which creates a realistic accidental disclosure risk. There is no evidence of network exfiltration or malicious intent, so the confidence is high for the behavior but moderate for exploitability.

content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to turn repeated AI workflows into clear, reusable skill packages. This skill provides a structured process, templates, validation, and packaging guidance for reliable skill creation.
actual_capabilities: Explains the structure of a valid skill package and required SKILL.md metadata.
Guides authors through examples, reusable resources, editing, packaging, and iteration.
Provides an initializer script that creates a skill folder with starter resources.
Provides a validator for basic frontmatter and naming checks.
Provides a packager that validates a skill and creates a distributable ZIP archive.
limitations: Validation is basic and does not prove that skill content is safe or high quality.
The initializer does not enforce the documented skill-name rules before writing files.
Packaging includes all files under the selected skill folder unless the author removes them first.
The guidance is focused on Claude-style skills and may need adaptation for other runtimes.

use_cases.title: Build a New Team Skill
use_cases.description: Create a reusable skill for a repeated workflow, including instructions, scripts, references, and assets.

use_cases.title: Standardize Skill Documentation
use_cases.description: Improve an existing skill so its trigger description, structure, and resource references are clear.

use_cases.title: Package a Skill for Sharing
use_cases.description: Validate a completed skill folder and produce a ZIP archive for distribution or review.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Skill
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner author creating a first skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new skill for this repeated task: [describe task]. Ask only the key questions first, then draft the skill structure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Reusable Resources
prompt_templates.scenario: Author deciding which files belong in a skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these example user requests and identify which instructions, scripts, references, and assets should be included in the skill.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Skill Content
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this skill for clear triggers, progressive disclosure, duplicated content, and missing resource references. Suggest focused edits.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare for Distribution
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced author packaging and reviewing a skill.
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate this skill folder for metadata, naming, resource organization, security concerns, and packaging readiness. Provide a release checklist.

output_examples.input: I need a skill for recurring invoice review tasks.
output_examples.output: A proposed skill outline with trigger description, workflow sections, required references, optional scripts, and first-draft authoring steps.

output_examples.input: Review my existing analytics skill before I share it.
output: A concise assessment of metadata quality and trigger clarity.
A list of resource files that should stay, move, or be removed.
A packaging checklist with validation and cleanup steps.

output_examples.input: Turn these repeated support workflows into a reusable skill.
output_examples.output: A skill design plan that separates core instructions, detailed references, helper scripts, and final packaging tasks.

best_practices: Start from real user requests before deciding the skill structure.
Keep SKILL.md concise and move detailed reference material into separate files.
Review generated packages for secrets, private files, and unused examples before sharing.
anti_patterns: Do not include large reference documents directly in SKILL.md when they can be loaded separately.
Do not keep placeholder scripts, references, or assets that the finished skill does not need.
Do not package a skill folder before checking the archive contents.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create AI skill packages with instructions, optional scripts, references, assets, validation, and packaging steps.

faq.question: Does it generate a starter skill folder?
faq.answer: Yes. The included initializer script creates a starter folder with SKILL.md and example resource directories.

faq.question: Can it validate finished skills?
faq.answer: It includes a basic validator for frontmatter, naming, and description checks. It is not a full security scanner.

faq.question: Can it package a skill for distribution?
faq.answer: Yes. The packager validates the folder and creates a ZIP archive that preserves the skill directory structure.

faq.question: What should users check before packaging?
faq.answer: Users should remove placeholders, secrets, private notes, hidden files, and unused generated artifacts before creating an archive.

faq.question: Is this only for Claude?
faq.answer: The guidance is written for Claude-style skills, but many authoring practices also apply to Codex and Claude Code workflows.
